skill.name: routeros-sniffer
skill.description: Guides RouterOS packet capture, TZSP streaming, mangle-based mirroring, and host-side analysis with Wireshark or tshark.
skill.summary: Capture and analyze RouterOS traffic with sniffer and TZSP workflows.
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, filesystem, and cryptography patterns, but most are markdown examples for RouterOS, tshark, Wireshark, QEMU, and firewall setup. No prompt injection or malicious intent was found in the reviewed files. The main confirmed risk is that packet capture and TZSP mirroring can expose sensitive network payloads if used outside an authorized troubleshooting scope.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Traffic Capture and Mirroring
medium_findings.description: The skill teaches RouterOS packet capture and TZSP mirroring. This is legitimate administration guidance, but captured packets may contain credentials, session data, or private traffic.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines directly configure live packet capture and TZSP streaming. The behavior is intended, but it creates a clear data exposure risk if misused.

low_findings.title: Host Firewall Changes Require Review
low_findings.description: The reference includes sudo commands that install tshark and open UDP capture ports. These are expected setup steps, but they change host security posture.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sudo commands are explicit and can affect host configuration. They are standard administrative commands, so the risk is contextual rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Temporary Capture Files Require Protection
low_findings.description: The reference saves packet captures under /tmp. This is a normal troubleshooting pattern, but capture files may contain sensitive payloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands write packet capture files to temporary paths. The risk is data handling, not arbitrary filesystem access or malicious persistence.

low_findings.title: Documented Commands Misclassified as Code Execution
low_findings.description: The numerous Ruby backtick execution alerts are false positives. The files are markdown documentation with shell, RouterOS, and filter examples, not executable Ruby source.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text appears inside markdown code fences and prose. No Ruby file, runtime hook, or automatic command execution path was found.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts are false positives caused by sniffer and protocol wording. No hashing, encryption, or authentication algorithm use was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines discuss packet sniffing and Wireshark support. No cryptographic API or weak algorithm implementation is present.

low_findings.title: Relative Links Misclassified as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The path traversal alerts are false positives. The parent-directory sequences are markdown links to neighboring skill documentation, not filesystem reads or writes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The references are static markdown links. There is no code path that accepts user input or resolves these links against the local filesystem.

low_findings.title: Example Network Targets Are Documentation Values
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs and IP addresses are example RouterOS, QEMU, DNS, and receiver targets. They do not indicate hidden callbacks or unauthorized exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network values are shown in setup examples and use placeholders or common lab addresses. No evidence of concealed external collection endpoints was found.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Match Intended Troubleshooting
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alerts are tied to packet filtering and capture guidance. This is the documented purpose, with no evidence of stealthy discovery behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe capture filtering and loop prevention. No commands enumerate host secrets, scan third-party networks, or hide discovery output.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Traffic Capture and Mirroring
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill teaches RouterOS packet capture and TZSP mirroring. This is legitimate administration guidance, but captured packets may contain credentials, session data, or private traffic.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines directly configure live packet capture and TZSP streaming. The behavior is intended, but it creates a clear data exposure risk if misused.

dangerous_patterns.title: Host Firewall Changes Require Review
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference includes sudo commands that install tshark and open UDP capture ports. These are expected setup steps, but they change host security posture.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sudo commands are explicit and can affect host configuration. They are standard administrative commands, so the risk is contextual rather than malicious.

content.value_statement: RouterOS packet debugging can be difficult when tcpdump is unavailable on the router. This skill provides practical sniffer, TZSP, and receiver workflows for authorized troubleshooting.
actual_capabilities: Explains RouterOS /tool/sniffer capture modes and key filters.
Shows how to stream live packets to a TZSP receiver.
Documents mangle action=sniff-tzsp for targeted packet mirroring.
Provides host-side Wireshark, tshark, and tcpdump receiver guidance.
Includes CHR and QEMU testing patterns for lab validation.
Lists cleanup steps for sniffer settings and temporary mangle rules.
limitations: Requires authorized access to a RouterOS device or lab image.
Does not decode captures inside the skill without external tools.
Packet visibility can be limited by hardware offload and wireless forwarding.
Captured traffic may contain sensitive data and must be handled carefully.

use_cases.title: Debug RouterOS Protocol Issues
use_cases.description: Capture ICMP, DNS, or TCP flows from RouterOS to confirm packet paths and protocol behavior.

use_cases.title: Build a CHR Packet Capture Lab
use_cases.description: Use QEMU CHR and tshark to test capture workflows without physical MikroTik hardware.

use_cases.title: Mirror Specific Traffic Safely
use_cases.description: Create focused mangle rules that copy selected flows to a receiver during incident analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Capture
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one RouterOS interface.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me capture ICMP traffic on ether1 with RouterOS sniffer and view it from the router console.

prompt_templates.title: Stream to Wireshark
prompt_templates.scenario: Send live TZSP packets to a workstation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to stream RouterOS sniffer traffic to Wireshark over TZSP on UDP port 37008.

prompt_templates.title: Mirror a Single Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Use firewall mangle for targeted capture.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a RouterOS mangle sniff-tzsp rule that mirrors DNS traffic from one client to my receiver.

prompt_templates.title: Validate a CHR Lab
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced lab workflow with QEMU CHR and tshark.
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan an end-to-end CHR lab test that configures TZSP streaming, generates traffic, captures with tshark, and cleans up.

output_examples.input: Capture ICMP packets from ether1 and stop after testing.
output_examples.output: A short RouterOS sniffer workflow with interface and protocol filters, start and stop steps, and cleanup notes.

output_examples.input: Receive TZSP packets on my Linux workstation.
output_examples.output: A host-side checklist covering tshark installation, the UDP capture filter, file output, and firewall allowance review.

output_examples.input: Mirror DNS from one LAN host only.
output_examples.output: A targeted mangle plan that matches the source host and DNS port, sends copies to the receiver, and removes the rule afterward.

best_practices: Capture only the interface, host, protocol, and time window needed for the task.
Use authorized lab or maintenance windows when packet payloads may include sensitive data.
Stop captures and remove temporary mangle rules immediately after validation.
anti_patterns: Do not mirror broad production traffic without written authorization and retention rules.
Do not leave TZSP streaming or firewall allow rules enabled after troubleshooting.
Do not store packet captures in shared locations without access controls.

faq.question: Does this skill run packet capture automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides instructions that a user or agent can apply in an authorized RouterOS environment.

faq.question: Which tools can receive TZSP packets?
faq.answer: The reference covers Wireshark for GUI analysis, tshark for CLI analysis, and tcpdump for raw UDP verification.

faq.question: Can it save captures to a router file?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents RouterOS sniffer file capture and download patterns for later analysis.

faq.question: Why use mangle action=sniff-tzsp?
faq.answer: Mangle rules can mirror specific flows without changing the original packets or capturing unrelated traffic.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: Packet captures can include credentials, private messages, and session data. Use narrow filters and protect saved files.

faq.question: Does it replace Wireshark or tshark?
faq.answer: No. It explains how to configure RouterOS and host receivers, but external tools perform capture decoding.
